content:
# Genesis Chapter 50 — LOGOS (Formal Equivalence) Translation

**Translator:** LOGOS Agent (Formal Equivalence)
**Sources:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folios 29r–29v (GEN_050_page_001.jpg, GEN_050_page_002.jpg)
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pages 50–51 (GEN_050_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, GEN_050_vaticanus_page_002.jpg)
**Date:** 2026-03-05
**Style:** Formal equivalence (ESV/NASB register); final chapter of Genesis

---

*Jacob's Death and Embalming*

**50:1** Then Joseph fell upon his father's face and wept over him and kissed him.

**50:2** And Joseph commanded his servants the physicians to embalm his father. So the physicians embalmed Israel.

**50:3** Forty days were spent for him — for such are the days required for embalming — and the Egyptians wept for him seventy days.

---

*The Journey to Canaan for Burial*

**50:4** And when the days of weeping for him had passed, Joseph spoke to the household of Pharaoh, saying, "If now I have found favor in your eyes, speak, I pray you, in the hearing of Pharaoh:

**50:5** 'My father made me swear an oath, saying, "Behold, I am about to die; in my tomb that I hewed out for myself in the land of Canaan, there you shall bury me." Now therefore, let me go up and bury my father. Then I will return.'"

**50:6** And Pharaoh said, "Go up and bury your father, as he made you swear."

**50:7** So Joseph went up to bury his father. And with him went up all the servants of Pharaoh, the elders of his household, and all the elders of the land of Egypt,

**50:8** as well as all the household of Joseph, his brothers, and his father's household. Only their little ones, their flocks, and their herds were left in the land of Goshen.

**50:9** And there went up with him both chariots and horsemen; it was a very great company.

**50:10** When they came to the threshing floor of Atad, which is beyond the Jordan, they lamented there with a very great and grievous lamentation, and he observed a mourning of seven days for his father.

**50:11** And when the inhabitants of the land, the Canaanites, saw the mourning at the threshing floor of Atad, they said, "This is a grievous mourning by the Egyptians." Therefore its name was called Abel-mizraim [*'Abel Mitsrayim* — "Meadow of Egypt" or "Mourning of Egypt"], which is beyond the Jordan.

**50:12** His sons did for him as he had commanded them:

**50:13** his sons carried him to the land of Canaan and buried him in the cave of the field at Machpelah, to the east of Mamre, which Abraham bought with the field for a burial possession from Ephron the Hittite.

---

*The Brothers Fear Joseph's Retribution*

**50:14** After he had buried his father, Joseph returned to Egypt — he and his brothers and all who had gone up with him to bury his father.

**50:15** When Joseph's brothers saw that their father was dead, they said, "It may be that Joseph harbors a grudge against us and will pay us back for all the evil we did to him."

**50:16** So they sent a message to Joseph, saying, "Your father gave this command before he died:

**50:17** 'Say to Joseph, Please forgive the transgression of your brothers and their sin, because they did evil to you.' And now, please forgive the transgression of the servants of the God of your father." Joseph wept when they spoke to him.

**50:18** His brothers also came and fell down before him and said, "Behold, we are your servants."

---

*Joseph's Answer: The Hermeneutical Key*

**50:19** But Joseph said to them, "Do not fear, for am I in the place of God?

**50:20** As for you, you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good, in order to bring it about that many people should be kept alive, as they are this day.

**50:21** So do not fear; I will provide for you and your little ones." Thus he comforted them and spoke to their hearts.

---

*Joseph's Final Years in Egypt*

**50:22** So Joseph remained in Egypt — he and his father's household. And Joseph lived one hundred and ten years.

**50:23** And Joseph saw Ephraim's children of the third generation. The children also of Machir the son of Manasseh were born on Joseph's knees.

**50:24** And Joseph said to his brothers, "I am about to die, but God will surely visit you and bring you up out of this land to the land that he swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob."

**50:25** Then Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath, saying, "God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from here."

**50:26** So Joseph died, being one hundred and ten years old. They embalmed him and he was placed in a coffin in Egypt.

---

## Translation Notes

### Key Term Choices

**v.1 — "fell upon his father's face" (wayyippol 'al-penei aviv)**
The Hebrew idiom is rendered literally. The LXX (ἐπέπεσεν ἐπὶ τὸ πρόσωπον τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ) follows suit. The gesture conveys immediate, uninhibited grief, the collapse of a son onto the face of his father rather than merely bowing before him.

**v.2 — "physicians" (haro-fim)**
The Egyptians are specified as physicians (*rof'im*, healers), not generic embalmers. The term preserves the dignity of the professional class employed for the task. The LXX uses ἐντεταφιαστάς (embalmers) for the second occurrence — a distinction noted under LXX Variants below.

**v.3 — "Forty days ... seventy days"**
Both figures are given without editorial comment. Forty days reflects Egyptian embalming practice; seventy days was the customary mourning period for royalty in Egypt. Jacob is given honor approaching that of Pharaoh's own household.

**v.5 — "my tomb that I hewed out for myself" (qivri asher kariti li)**
The verb karah (to dig, hew) reflects Jacob's active preparation of his burial site. Some traditions read this as the cave of Machpelah, though it was purchased (not dug) by Abraham. The LXX renders it simply ὃν ὤρυξα ἐμαυτῷ (which I dug for myself), following the same idiom. The difficulty may reflect Jacob's sense of ownership — the ancestral tomb he secured by covenant.

**v.10 — "threshing floor of Atad" (goren ha-atad)**
Atad means "bramble" or "thornbush." The LXX renders the location as ἅλω Αταδ. The location "beyond the Jordan" (me-ever ha-yarden) points east of the Jordan River — a detour that may reflect the need to avoid Philistine territory or honor a specific road of mourning.

**v.11 — Abel-mizraim (etymology preserved)**
*'Abel* can mean either "meadow" (from a different root) or "mourning" (from 'aval). The wordplay on Egypt's mourning is preserved in the gloss. The LXX uses Πένθος Αἰγύπτου (Mourning of Egypt), reading the mourning sense.

**v.17 — "transgression" (pesha')**
*Pesha'* is the strongest term in the Hebrew vocabulary of sin — willful rebellion or breach of a covenant relationship. "Transgression" (following ESV) preserves this weight over "sin" (*het'*), which also appears in the same verse (rendered "sin"). The distinction between pesha' and het' marks a deliberate escalation: the brothers acknowledge both willful rebellion and inadvertent failure.

**v.19 — "Am I in the place of God?" (ha-tachat Elohim ani)**
The word order places the emphatic pronoun *ani* (I) at the end: "Is it in the place of God — I?" The question is rhetorical and humble: Joseph refuses to assume divine prerogative for judgment. The LXX (μὴ γὰρ ἀντὶ θεοῦ ἐγώ εἰμι;) follows the same structure. This question is the theological hinge of v.19–20.

**v.20 — "you meant evil ... God meant it for good" (atem hashavtem 'alai ra'ah, Elohim hashav otah le-tovah)**
The pivotal verb *hashav* (to reckon, intend, devise) is used twice in deliberate parallelism. The same calculating intention — *hashav* — is attributed first to the brothers (evil intent) and then to God (good intent) working through the same sequence of events. "Meant" preserves this active-intentional force better than "planned" or "intended." The clause is not merely consolatory but cosmic: God's hidden sovereignty re-purposes human malice without excusing it.

**v.20 — "to bring it about that many people should be kept alive" (le-ma'an 'asot ka-yom ha-zeh le-ha'achayot am rav)**
*Le-ha'achayot* is the Hiphil infinitive of *chayah* — "to cause to live, to preserve alive." "Kept alive" follows the ESV; "preserved alive" (NASB) is equally valid. "Many people" (*am rav*, literally "a great people") renders the scope as national/international rather than merely familial.

**v.24 — "God will surely visit you" (paqod yifqod Elohim etchem)**
The infinitive absolute (*paqod*) before the finite verb (*yifqod*) signals emphatic certainty in Biblical Hebrew: "He will visit — yes, He will visit." English lacks an exact equivalent; "surely" is the conventional rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV). The verb *paqad* (to visit, attend to, appoint) carries the nuance of divine attentiveness that will culminate in the Exodus. The same construction and promise is repeated in v.25.

**v.25 — "carry up my bones from here" (va-ha'alitem et-'atsmotai mizzeh)**
The verb is the Hiphil of *'alah* (to go up) — the same root as the great Exodus verb. Joseph's bones become a living pledge of the promise: they will "go up" with Israel when God "visits." This oath is fulfilled in Exodus 13:19 and Joshua 24:32.

**v.26 — "coffin" (aron)**
*Aron* is the same word used for the Ark of the Covenant. Most scholars distinguish the usages by context; nonetheless the lexical connection is noted. The final word of Genesis is not a land or a promise but a coffin. This is the controlled ending: Joseph is embalmed, like Jacob before him, and placed (*wayyissem*) in a coffin in Egypt. Genesis ends in Egypt. The promise of the land is deferred. The "surely visit" of v.24–25 now hangs over the silence.

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus)

**v.2 — "physicians" / "embalmers"**
The MT uses *ha-rof'im* (physicians) in the first occurrence and implies the same in the second. The LXX uses τοῖς ἐντεταφιαστάς (the embalmers/mummifiers) for the servants commanded to embalm, while using ἰατροί (physicians) for the physicians who carried out the embalming. Vaticanus thus distinguishes the professionals more sharply than the MT, reflecting knowledge of Egyptian practice.

**v.3 — "forty days" / duration**
The LXX follows the MT exactly: forty days for embalming, seventy for mourning. No variant.

**v.10 — "threshing floor of Atad"**
The LXX renders *goren ha-atad* as ἅλω Αταδ, transliterating the personal name. This is consistent with the MT.

**v.11 — "Abel-mizraim"**
Where the MT's wordplay allows either "meadow" or "mourning," the LXX resolves clearly for Πένθος Αἰγύπτου (Mourning of Egypt), eliminating the ambiguity of the MT. Vaticanus follows this reading.

**v.16 — "They sent a message" vs. "They commanded"**
The MT reads *wayyetsavvu* (and they commanded/sent an order), implying a formal instruction. The LXX (Vaticanus: εἴπαν... τῷ Ἰωσήφ) reads more simply as "they said to Joseph," slightly softening the formal framing. This may be a harmonization toward narrative smoothness.

**v.20 — "you meant evil ... God meant it for good"**
The LXX renders this with ὑμεῖς ἐβουλεύσασθε κατ' ἐμοῦ εἰς πονηρά, ὁ δὲ θεὸς ἐβουλεύσατο περὶ ἐμοῦ εἰς ἀγαθά — using βουλεύομαι (to counsel, deliberate, purpose) rather than the more calculating *hashav* nuance of the MT. The shift from "reckon/devise" to "deliberate/counsel" slightly softens the parallel but preserves the double-agent structure of the verse.

**v.23 — "born on Joseph's knees"**
The MT: *yulledu 'al-birkei Yosef* (were born on the knees of Joseph). The LXX follows: ἐτέχθησαν ἐπὶ μηρῶν Ιωσηφ (were born on the thighs of Joseph). The LXX's word choice (μηρῶν, thighs) may reflect a slightly different anatomical idiom for the adoption gesture, or a euphemistic adjustment.

**v.26 — Final verse**
The LXX closes identically to the MT in substance: Joseph dies at 110 years, is embalmed, and is placed in a coffin (σορός) in Egypt. No divergence. Both traditions conclude Genesis on the same note of suspended promise.

---

## Theological Note: A Coffin in Egypt

Genesis ends not with the Promised Land, not with a covenant ceremony, not even with Joseph's death itself, but with a single, spare image: a coffin in Egypt.

The final Hebrew clause is five words: *wayyissem ba-aron be-Mitsrayim* — "and he was placed in a coffin in Egypt." This is the terminal period of the book. After the vast sweep of creation, flood, covenant, patriarchal wandering, famine, and reunion, Genesis closes in a box in a foreign country.

This ending is not pessimistic; it is eschatological. The coffin is the hinge between two books. Joseph's bones will not rest in Egypt; they cannot, because of the oath he extracted from his brothers (v.25). The coffin in Egypt is Genesis's final act of forward pressure — a promissory note awaiting collection. As long as those bones lie in that box, the story of Israel is unfinished.

Joseph's speech in v.19–20 provides the hermeneutical key not only to the Joseph narrative but to the whole of Genesis: human evil and divine purpose are not simply compatible — they are, in the hands of God, convertible. The brothers' malice was real. The suffering was real. And yet the same divine *hashav* (reckoning, intent) that works through all of Genesis — through Cain and Abel, through the flood, through Babel, through Abraham's near-sacrifice of Isaac — operates here with full transparency. For once, the character inside the story sees it. Joseph sees Providence, and names it.

The theological movement of Genesis runs from the garden to the coffin. Both are placed in locations that are not yet home. Adam is cast east of Eden; Joseph is sealed east of the promise. But both endings face forward. The garden awaits a new creation; the coffin awaits an exodus. Genesis is not complete — it is a promissory text, a book that ends by pointing beyond itself.

*Sof Sefer Bereshit.* The book of Genesis is completed.

---
